name: D3.js (Data-Driven Documents)

performance.notes: SVG rendering; Canvas for large datasets; WebGL (via regl/deck.gl) for millions of points

agent_readiness.notes: The foundational data visualization library for the web. Binds data to DOM elements, applies transformations, and renders SVG, Canvas, or HTML. Scales (linear, log, time, ordinal), axes, shapes (line, area, arc, symbol), layouts (force, tree, hierarchy, pack, chord), projections for maps, transitions/animations. Low-level but maximally flexible. Every chart type possible. Steep learning curve. Powers many higher-level libraries (Vega, Observable Plot, Nivo). v7 modular (import individual packages).

gotchas: Steep learning curve — DOM manipulation paradigm differs from React/Vue
Verbose code for common chart types (Plotly/Vega simpler for standard charts)
React/Angular integration is awkward (use wrappers like Nivo, Recharts)
Complex animations require deep D3 knowledge
best_when: Custom, unique visualizations not possible with charting libraries
Interactive data stories with complex user interactions
Teams with dedicated frontend + data visualization expertise
avoid_when: Standard chart types (bar, line, pie) — use Plotly, Chart.js, or Recharts
React apps without D3 experience (use Nivo or Victory instead)
summary: D3.js is the foundational web data visualization library — maximum flexibility for custom, unique visualizations. Binds data to DOM (SVG/Canvas/HTML), applies scales/axes/shapes, and handles complex layouts and animations. Powers Vega, Observable Plot, and many charting libraries. Steep learning curve worth it only for truly custom visualizations. Use Plotly, Recharts, or Nivo for standard chart types.
